coredata.dc:title: Dual-emission carbon dots for ratiometric detection of Fe3+ ions and acid phosphatase
coredata.dc:creator: Pang, Shu
Liu, Siyu
coredata.dc:description: We have developed a simple and convenient route to prepare fluorescent carbon dots with dual emission peaks respectively at 470 and 570 nm. The prepared dual-emission carbon dots can be used for ratiometric detection of Fe3+ ions in the range from 0 to 50 μmol·L-1 with 0.8 μmol·L-1 detection limit based on the fluorescence quenching at 570 nm. The quenched fluorescence induced by Fe3+ ions could be recovered by pyrophosphate. We further used the carbon dots-Fe3+ ions-pyrophosphate mixed system for ratiometric detection of acid phosphatase in the range from 0.08 to 6.75 μg·mL-1 with 0.01 μg·mL-1 detection limit.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Dual emission
Ratiometric
Fe3+ ions
Acid phosphatase

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Fe3+ ions based on GN-CDs
Detection of ACP based on GN-CDs
The preparation and characterization of GN-CDs
The fluorescent ratiometric detection of Fe3+ ions based on GN-CDs
The fluorescent quenching mechanism of GN-CDs induced by Fe3+ ions
The ratiometric detection of ACP based on GN-CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Scientific Research Foundation of Liaoning Shihua University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Scientific Research Foundation of Liaoning Shihua University .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs, G-CDs and GN-CDs upon 360 nm excitations. Inset: The photos of N-CDs (a), G-CDs (b) and GN-CDs (c) solutions under daylight or UV light. (B) The UV–Vis absorption spectra of N-CDs, G-CDs and GN-CDs.

(A) The fluorescence emission intensity at 470 and 570 nm of GN-CDs solutions in various pH environments (pH 10.2, 9.4, 8.6, 7.8, 7.0 6.2, 5.4, 4.6, 3.8). (B) The fluorescence emission spectra of GN-CDs solutions in various pH environments (pH 10.2, 9.8, 9.4, 9.0, 8.6, 8.2, 7.8, 7.4, 7.0, 6.6, 6.2).
F470, F570, and ratio F470/F570 of GN-CDs solutions incubated with individual metal ions including 0.2 mmol L−1 Ba2+, Al3+, Co2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Cr2O7 2−, Mg2+, Cd2+, Na+, K+, Cu2+, or Fe3+ ions.

(A)The fluorescence emission spectra of GN-CDs in the presence of various Fe3+ ions concentrations (a: 0, b: 4, c: 10, d: 15, e: 20, f: 30, g: 40, h: 50, i: 60, j: 80, k: 100 μmol L−1). Inset: The plot relationship between Fe3+ ions concentrations and ratios of F470/F570. (B) The UV–Vis absorption spectra of GN-CDs in the presence of various Fe3+ ions concentrations (a: 0, b: 4, c: 10, d: 15, e: 20, f: 30, g: 40, h: 50, i: 60, j: 80, k: 100 μmol L−1). Photos: The images of GN-CDs solutions respectively with 0 μmol L−1 (a), 15 μmol L−1 (b), 40 μmol L−1 (c), 100 μmol L−1 (d) Fe3+ ions under daylight or ultraviolet light.

The fluorescence emission spectra of GN-CDs incubated with 150 μmol L−1 Fe3+ ions and 300 μmol L−1 sodium pyrophosphate in the presence of various ACP concentrations (a: 0, b: 0.08, c: 0.2, d: 0.5, e: 1, f: 2.5, g: 6.75, h: 12.5, i: 25 μg mL−1) Inset: The plot relationship between ratios of F470/F570 and ACP concentrations.
The illustration of the synthesis and applications of GN-CDs.

head.ce:title: Dual-emission carbon dots for ratiometric detection of Fe3+ ions and acid phosphatase

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Environmental Engineering, Liaoning Shihua University, Fushun, 113001, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of chemistry, chemical engineering and environmental engineering, Liaoning Shihua University, Fushun, 113001, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life and Health Sciences, Northeastern University, Shenyang, 110000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Life and Health Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Life and Health Sciences, Northeastern University, Shenyang, 110000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We have developed a simple and convenient route to prepare fluorescent carbon dots with dual emission peaks respectively at 470 and 570 nm. The prepared dual-emission carbon dots can be used for ratiometric detection of Fe3+ ions in the range from 0 to 50 μmol·L-1 with 0.8 μmol·L-1 detection limit based on the fluorescence quenching at 570 nm. The quenched fluorescence induced by Fe3+ ions could be recovered by pyrophosphate. We further used the carbon dots-Fe3+ ions-pyrophosphate mixed system for ratiometric detection of acid phosphatase in the range from 0.08 to 6.75 μg·mL-1 with 0.01 μg·mL-1 detection limit.

ce:section.ce:para: Ferrum is an essential trace element in human body, which plays a pivotal role in oxygen metabolism and electron transfer processes in all organisms [1,2]. The deficiency and excess accumulation of Fe3+ ions can both disturb the cellular homeostasis, and induce a variety of biological dysfunction, including liver injury, renal failure, and etc [3,4]. Besides, iron pollution in water environment has posed serious threat to the environment safety. The iron content in drinking water cannot exceed 5.357 μmol L−1 stipulated by United States Environmental Protection Agency [5,6]. Acid phosphatases (ACP), a digestive enzyme widely existed in nature, can effectively remove a phosphate group from its substrate in acidic environment [7,8]. Although ACP level is low in human body, the abnormal level of ACP is involved in lots of diseases, such as prostate cancer, Gaucher disease, and etc [9–12]. Due to the important impact on living organisms and environment, to develop a sensitive and convenient method for detection of Fe3+ ions and ACP are very necessary.

Ratiometric fluorescent assay is a special detection method based on the ratio changes of the fluorescence intensities at various wavelengths. The ratio of fluorescence is unaffected by the fluctuation of light source and the interference of environment noises. Until now, most of the reported fluorescent probes with dual emissions were made up of organic dyes, quantum dots, metal nanoclusters, or carbon nanomaterials [13–20]. Fluorescent carbon dots, as a burgeoning carbon nanomaterial, are very suitable for the manufacture of ratiometric fluorescent probe due to their tunable fluorescent emission wavelength, wide raw materials sources, excellent biocompatibility and photostability [21,22]. However, complex prepared steps and long consuming time limited their further applications. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a simple and convenient route to prepare dual-emission carbon dots.

In this work, we used gallic acid and o-phenylenediamine as raw materials to prepare dual-emission carbon dots (GN-CDs) that displayed two fluorescent emission peaks respectively at 470 and 570 nm Fe3+ ions can quench solely the fluorescence peak at 570 nm of GN-CDs. Based on the strong binding ability of pyrophosphate to Fe3+ ions and ACP-catalyzed hydrolysis of pyrophosphate, we can further use the GN-CDs-Fe3+ ions-pyrophosphate mixed system for ratiometric detection of ACP.

The fluorescence and ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were recorded on a TECAN infinite M100 PRO microplate reader. The dynamic laser scattering and zeta potential measurements were operated on a Malvern Nano ZS90 instrument. FT-IR spectra were recorded with a Bruker IFS66V FT-IR spectrometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained by a TECNAI G2 20 TEM. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured by ESCALAB 250Xi.

Gallic acid, o-phenylenediamine, acid phosphatase, various metal salts and etc. were obtained from Bejing Dingguo Genetech Co., Ltd. All chemicals used in the experiments were of analytical grade. The used deionized water had a resistivity higher than 18 MΩ cm−1.

GN-CDs were synthesized as follows: 0.5 mmol of gallic acid and o-phenylenediamine were dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water. The obtained solution was transferred into a 25 mL high pressure reactor, which was kept in an oven with 180 °C for 4 h. The reactor was naturally cooled down to room temperature. The supernatant was collected after centrifugation with the speed of 12, 000 rpm, and further purified by dialysis tubing with a molecule weight cut of 500 Da. The purified GN-CDs solution was freeze-dried into powder. The concentration of purified GN-CDs solution used in the following process was about 2.9 mg mL−1.

The fluorescent carbon dots prepared with o-phenylenediamine as raw material was defined as N-CDs. The fluorescent carbon dots prepared with gallic acid as raw material was defined as G-CDs. The N-CDs or G-CDs were prepared similar to above procedure. Briefly, 0.5 mmol of o-phenylenediamine or 0.5 mmol of gallic acid was dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water. The obtained solution was transferred into the high pressure reactor. After being heated at 180 °C for 4 h, the reactor was naturally cooled down to room temperature. The supernatant was collected after centrifugation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ ions based on GN-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: For the detection of Fe3+ ions, 40 μL of tri-hydroxymethyl aminomethane-HCl (Tris-HCl) buffer solution (100 mmol L−1, pH 5.4), 60 μL of purified GN-CDs solution, and varying amounts of ferric chloride solution were mixed, and then diluted with deionized water to 200 μL. After incubation of 5 min at room temperature, the fluorescence spectra and UV–Vis spectroscopy of the solution were recorded and used for quantitative analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ACP based on GN-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The ACP detection was carried out as follows: 40 μL of Tris-HCl buffer solution (100 mmol L−1, pH 5.4), 60 μL of purified GN-CDs solution, a certain amount of ferric chloride and sodium pyrophosphate, and varying amounts of ACP were mixed, and then diluted with deionized water to 200 μL. The mixture was thoroughly shaken and equilibrated for 60 min at room temperature. The fluorescence spectra and UV–Vis spectroscopy of the solution were recorded and used for quantitative analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The preparation and characterization of GN-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As described in Scheme 1 , we prepared a dual-emission fluorescent carbon dots GN-CDs by heating o-phenylenediamine and gallic acid in aqueous solutions. The fluorescence spectra of N-CDs, G-CDs, and GN-CDs were shown in Fig. 1 A. The N-CDs only had a single fluorescence peak around 570 nm, and G-CDs displayed a single fluorescence peak around 460 nm. The prepared GN-CDs displayed two emission peaks respectively at 470 nm and 570 nm upon 360 nm excitations. Compared with N-CDs and G-CDs, the emerging absorption band at 360 nm and 370 nm of GN-CDs can be attributed to the n-π* transitions of heteroatom (N and O)-containing bonds (Fig. 1B) [23,24]. We further compared the zeta-potentials of N-CDs, G-CDs and GN-CDs, and the GN-CDs exhibited more negative charges than N-CDs and G-CDs (Fig. S1). The FT-IR spectra of the GN-CDs displayed the stretching vibrations for N–H/O–H (3225 cm−1), C–O (1120 cm−1), C–N (1230 cm−1), C–N= (1335 cm−1), vibrations of amide’s CO and C–N (1670 cm−1, 1405 cm−1), out-of-plane vibrations of the benzene ring (760 cm−1, 715 cm−1), indicating the formation of polyaromatic structures and rich nitrogen/oxygen -containing groups on the surface of GN-CDs (Fig. S2) [25,26]. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was measured to further characterize the structural components of GN-CDs (Fig. S3). The XPS full scan spectrum of GN-CDs indicated three predominant peaks at 285, 399 and 532 eV, corresponding to the binding energies of C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively [27–29]. The elemental analysis indicated the elementary composition of GN-CDs was presented as follows: 76.84% (C), 14.21% (N) and 8.95% (O), respectively. The detailed XPS spectra of C 1s (Fig. S3B), N 1s (Fig. S3C), and O 1s (Fig. S3D) further demonstrated the existence of CC, C–N, CO, C–O, C–O–C, and N–H bonds in GN-CDs [27,29].

Fig. S4 indicated that the prepared GN-CDs had a size distribution in the range from 1.82 to 4.31 nm with 3.21 nm mean size. The fluorescence excitation spectra of GN-CDs respectively with 470 nm or 570 nm emission wavelengths had some overlaps around 360 nm (Fig. S5). Therefore, we selected 360 nm as excitation wavelengths to investigate the dual-emission intensity changes of GN-CDs in the further experiments.

We respectively investigated the stability of GN-CDs against photobleaching and temperature variations (Fig. S6 and Fig. S7). Fig. S6 showed that the fluorescence emission intensity at 570 nm (F570) of GN-CDs was almost constant in continuous ultraviolet irradiation for 90 min, and the fluorescence emission intensity at 470 nm (F470) declined slightly. F470 and F570 of GN-CDs both remained unchanged at different temperature in the range from 25 to 55 °C (Fig. S7). The effects of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and salts concentrations on GN-CDs were further explored. F470 and F570 of GN-CDs can remain stable even incubated with 250 μmol L−1 H2O2 or 500 mmol L−1 NaCl/KCl for 30 min incubations (Fig. S8 and Fig. S9). Furthermore, the fluorescence of GN-CDs displayed excellent stability in the presence of various biomolecules including hemin, methione, arginine, threonine, sucrose, glutamate, l-cysteine, dopamine, pepsine, trypsin or bovine serum albumin (BSA) (Fig. S10). Due to the difference of preparation methods, the fluorescent response of various carbon dots to pH environments are usually complicated [30–32]. Fig. 2 A described the F470 and F570 of GN-CDs in various pH environments (pH 3.8 to pH 10.2). The dual emission intensity of GN-CDs remained unchanged in the pH range from 3.8 to 6.2. Fig. 2B exhibited the fluorescent changes of GN-CDs in the range from 6.2 to 10.2. We can see that F570 reduced dramatically with the rise of pH value from 6.2 to 9.0, whereas F470 decreased slightly with the red shift of emission peak. A new absorbing peak around 455 nm appeared gradually with the increase of pH value from 6.2 to 10.2 (Fig. S11). The deprotonation of surface groups of GN-CDs was deepening along with the rise of pH value. Therefore, we utilized pH 5.4 buffer solutions to maintain the pH of systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fluorescent ratiometric detection of Fe3+ ions based on GN-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
We further investigated the fluorescent response of GN-CDs to various metal ions. Fig. 3 showed that most of metal ions had a slight effect on the dual emission intensity of GN-CDs, and only Fe3+ ions can quench markedly F570 to increase the ratio F470/F570. Herein, we investigated systematically the effects of Fe3+ ion concentrations on GN-CDs. Fig. 4 A offered the fluorescence emission spectra of GN-CDs solutions in the presence of different Fe3+ ions concentrations. We can find that the fluorescence emission peak at 570 nm of GN-CDs reduced dramatically with increasing of Fe3+ ions concentration from 0 to 100 μmol L−1, whereas the fluorescence emission peak at 470 nm slightly lowered. Fig.4AInset described the relationship between Fe3+ ions concentration and ratios of F470/F570 in the range from 0 to 50 μmol L−1, which was in accord with the following equations:
(1) F470/F570 = 0.4538 + 0.0109 [Fe3+]

The correlation coefficients is 0.998. The detection limit for Fe3+ ions is calculated to be 0.8 μmol L−1 (S/N = 3). Fig. 4B displayed the absorbance changes of GN-CDs solutions with different Fe3+ ions concentrations. It can be seen that the absorbance of GN-CDs solutions in the range from 350 to 850 nm enhanced overall after the addition of Fe3+ ions, and no new absorption peak was identified. The GN-CDs solutions changed from brown-yellow to dark brown under daylight with Fe3+ ions concentrations enhancement, and the GN-CDs solutions changed from yellow to black-purple under ultraviolet light (Fig.4BPhotos). Therefore, the prepared GN-CDs can be used as a fluorescent ratiometric probe for detection of Fe3+ ions. As shown in Table S1, we summarized various fluorescent probes for Fe3+ ions detection. Compared to previous reports, this method offered a simple route to prepare ratiometric fluorescent probe GN-CDs for detection of Fe3+ ions, and had a similar or excellent detection limit and dynamic range for Fe3+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fluorescent quenching mechanism of GN-CDs induced by Fe3+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: According to some previous reports, Fe3+ ions can bridge several adjacent carbon dots to form aggregates by the special coordination interaction between Fe3+ and phenolic hydroxyl groups of carbon dots, which lead to fluorescence quenching [33,34]. Herein, we further verified the quenching mechanism of GN-CDs by Fe3+ ions in detail. The zeta-potential changes of GN-CDs with various Fe3+ ions concentrations were found in Fig. S12. It can be seen that the negative charges of GN-CDs were neutralized gradually incubated with higher Fe3+ ions concentrations. At the same time, the hydrophilic size of GN-CDs increased gradually as Fe3+ ions concentrations increased, which indicated that Fe3+ ions induced the aggregations of GN-CDs (Fig. S13). The TEM images of GN-CDs with 40 μmol L−1 Fe3+ ions were shown in Fig. S14. We can see that the GN-CDs are prone to aggregate and stack in the presence of Fe3+ ions, which further proved the aggregations of GN-CDs induced by Fe3+ ions.

The fluorescence life of GN-CDs without or with Fe3+ ions were offered in Fig. S15. It can be found that the fluorescence life of GN-CDs (emission at 470 nm upon 360 nm excitation) almost kept the same after the addition of 40 μmol L−1 Fe3+, whereas the fluorescence life of GN-CDs (emission at 570 nm upon 360 nm excitation) significantly reduced from 3.51 to 2.82 ns after the addition of Fe3+ ions. The electron-transfer process between the Fe3+ and GN-CDs resulted in the fluorescence quenching at 570 nm to reduce their fluorescence lifetime [35]. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching at 570 nm of GN-CDs induced by Fe3+ ions can be attributed to a dynamic quenching processes. Fig. S16 showed the fluorescent response of GN-CDs to 100 μmol L−1 Fe3+ ions in the presence of 100 μmol L−1 various metal ions, which demonstrated the detection system based on GN-CDs had strong anti-interference capability for Fe3+ ions detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The ratiometric detection of ACP based on GN-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Because of the strong interaction between Fe3+ ions and phosphate groups, we studied the effects of monophosphate and pyrophosphate on the fluorescence of GN-CDs. As described in Fig. S17, sodium phosphate in the concentration range from 0 to 300 μmol L−1 had slight effects on the quenched fluorescence of GN-CDs-Fe3+ ions mixed solutions, whereas sodium pyrophosphate can effectively recover the quenched fluorescence of GN-CDs-Fe3+ ions mixed solutions. The above results demonstrated that pyrophosphate exhibited more powerful Fe3+-chelating ability than monophosphate. ACP can catalyze the hydrolysis of pyrophosphate to generate monophosphate. Therefore, the GN-CDs-Fe3+ ions-sodium pyrophosphate mixed system had the potential to be used for ACP detection.

Fig. S18 described the relationship between the ratio F470/F570 of GN-CDs-Fe3+ ions-sodium pyrophosphate mixed system and the incubation time after the addition of various ACP concentrations. The ratio F470/F570 increased gradually over time after the addition of ACP, which indicated that pyrophosphate underwent hydrolysis catalyzed by ACP, and Fe3+ ions were released to quench the fluorescence intensity at 570 nm of GN-CDs again. The effects of ACP concentrations on the GN-CDs-Fe3+ ions-sodium pyrophosphate mixed system were systematically investigated. With the enhancement of ACP concentrations in the range from 0 to 25 μg mL−1, the fluorescence emission peak at 570 nm gradually weakened, and the fluorescence emission peak at 470 nm varied slightly (Fig. 5 ). The relationship between ratio F470/F570 and the logarithm of ACP concentration in the range from 0.08 to 6.75 μg mL−1 can be described as the following equations:
(2) F470/F570 = 0.4538 + 0.0109Lg[ACP], μg·mL−1
The correlation coefficients is 0.996 with 0.01 μg mL−1 detection limit for ACP.

Fig. S19 offered the UV–Vis absorption spectra of GN-CDs-Fe3+ ions-sodium pyrophosphate mixed system incubated with various ACP concentrations. We can see that the absorbance of mixed system enhanced overall in the visible region with the increase of ACP concentrations, and the rising trend was similar to Fig. 4B. Therefore, the ACP detection mechanism was based on the hydrolysis of pyrophosphate catalyzed by ACP.

In order to test the feasibility of the proposed method in real samples detection, the GN-CDs were utilized for the determination of Fe3+ ions in tap water and spring water samples with the standard addition method. Different concentrations of Fe3+ ions were added to 5 times dilution water samples, and the detection results were offered in Table S2. The recoveries of Fe3+ ions based on this method were in the range from 103% to 110% with the relative standard deviations (RSD) no more than 4.9%. The healthy man’s urine were subjected to a 10-fold dilution, and spiked with various ACP concentrations. The recoveries of ACP based on the developed method in urine samples were in the range from 90% to 93% with the RSD no more than 4.7% (Table S3). The above results demonstrated the potential of this method for the detection of Fe3+ ions and ACP in real samples.

We prepared the dual-emission fluorescent carbon dots by adopting gallic acid and o-phenylenediamine as raw materials, which displayed two fluorescent emission peaks respectively at 470 and 570 nm upon 360 nm excitations. The prepared GN-CDs can act as a fluorescence ratiometric probe for Fe3+ ions detection based on the changes of the ratio F470/F570. The GN-CDs could be also used for ratiometric detection of ACP in the presence of Fe3+ ions and pyrophosphate based on the hydrolysis of ACP. Finally, the proposed assay based on GN-CDs was successfully applied for the detection of Fe3+ ions and ACP in real samples.
This work was supported by the Scientific Research Foundation of Liaoning Shihua University.
Shu Pang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Siyu Liu: Data curation, Investigation, Writing - original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2020.01.033.
